question_html: If A{tex}\cdot{/tex}(adj A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then the value of |A| + |adj A| is equal to:
question_text: If A {tex}\cdot{/tex} (adj A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then the value of |A| + |adj A| is equal to:

options.explanation: 12 Explaination: A {tex}\cdot{/tex} (adj A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} we know that A.(AdjA) = I.|A| {tex}\implies{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = |A| I {tex}\implies{/tex} 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = |A| I {tex}\implies{/tex} 3I = |A| I {tex}\implies{/tex} |A|=3 ---(1) |Adj A| = |A| 3-1 [ Since order n=3] |Adj A| = (3) 2 =9 |adj(A)| = 9 ------(2) Now, |A| + |adj A| = 3 + 9 =12

explanation: 12 Explaination: A {tex}\cdot{/tex} (adj A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} we know that A.(AdjA) = I.|A| {tex}\implies{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = |A| I {tex}\implies{/tex} 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = |A| I {tex}\implies{/tex} 3I = |A| I {tex}\implies{/tex} |A|=3 ---(1) |Adj A| = |A| 3-1 [ Since order n=3] |Adj A| = (3) 2 =9 |adj(A)| = 9 ------(2) Now, |A| + |adj A| = 3 + 9 =12

question_html: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \text x \\ \text y \\ \text z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 6 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then the value of (2x + y - z) is:
question_text: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \text x \\ \text y \\ \text z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 6 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then the value of (2x + y - z) is:

options.explanation: 5 Explanation {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \text x \\ \text y \\ \text z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 6 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x+y+z \\ y+z\\ z\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 6 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x+y+z =6 ------(i) y+z = 3 -----------(ii) z=2 --------------(iii) from (ii) and (iii) y + 2 = 3 y = 1 x+ y+ z = 6 x + 3 = 6 x=3 (2x + y - z) = 2(3)+1-2 = 6+1-2 = 7-2 =5

explanation: 5 Explanation {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \text x \\ \text y \\ \text z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 6 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x+y+z \\ y+z\\ z\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 6 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x+y+z =6 ------(i) y+z = 3 -----------(ii) z=2 --------------(iii) from (ii) and (iii) y + 2 = 3 y = 1 x+ y+ z = 6 x + 3 = 6 x=3 (2x + y - z) = 2(3)+1-2 = 6+1-2 = 7-2 =5

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Prove that the determinant {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{\sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{ - x}&1 \\ {\cos \theta }&1&x \end{array}} \right|{/tex} is independent of {tex}\theta {/tex}.
question_text: Prove that the determinant {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{\sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{ - x}&1 \\ {\cos \theta }&1&x \end{array}} \right|{/tex} is independent of {tex}\theta {/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\Delta = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{\sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{ - x}&1 \\ {\cos \theta }&1&x \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Expanding along first row, {tex}\Delta = x\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - x}&1 \\ 1&x \end{array}} \right| - \sin \theta \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - \sin \theta }&1 \\ {\cos \theta }&x \end{array}} \right| {/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - \sin \theta }&{ - x} \\ {\cos \theta }&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta = x\left( { - {x^2} - 1} \right) - \sin \theta \left( { - x\sin \theta - \cos \theta } \right) {/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta \left( { - \sin \theta + x\cos \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta = - {x^3} - x + x{\sin ^2}\theta {/tex} {tex}+ \sin \theta \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \theta + x{\cos ^2}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta = - {x^3} - x + x\left( {{{\sin }^2}\theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right) {/tex} = -x 3 - x + x = - x 3 which is independent of {tex}\theta {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\Delta = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{\sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{ - x}&1 \\ {\cos \theta }&1&x \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Expanding along first row, {tex}\Delta = x\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - x}&1 \\ 1&x \end{array}} \right| - \sin \theta \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - \sin \theta }&1 \\ {\cos \theta }&x \end{array}} \right| {/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - \sin \theta }&{ - x} \\ {\cos \theta }&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta = x\left( { - {x^2} - 1} \right) - \sin \theta \left( { - x\sin \theta - \cos \theta } \right) {/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta \left( { - \sin \theta + x\cos \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta = - {x^3} - x + x{\sin ^2}\theta {/tex} {tex}+ \sin \theta \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \theta + x{\cos ^2}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta = - {x^3} - x + x\left( {{{\sin }^2}\theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right) {/tex} = -x 3 - x + x = - x 3 which is independent of {tex}\theta {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\Delta = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{\sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{ - x}&1 \\ {\cos \theta }&1&x \end{array}} \right]{/tex} 

Expanding along first row,

{tex}\Delta = x\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - x}&1 \\ 1&x \end{array}} \right| - \sin \theta \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - \sin \theta }&1 \\ {\cos \theta }&x \end{array}} \right| {/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - \sin \theta }&{ - x} \\ {\cos \theta }&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta = x\left( { - {x^2} - 1} \right) - \sin \theta \left( { - x\sin \theta - \cos \theta } \right) {/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta \left( { - \sin \theta + x\cos \theta } \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta = - {x^3} - x + x{\sin ^2}\theta {/tex} {tex}+ \sin \theta \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \theta + x{\cos ^2}\theta{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta = - {x^3} - x + x\left( {{{\sin }^2}\theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right) {/tex} = -x3 - x + x = - x3 which is independent of {tex}\theta {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Gautam buys 5 pens, 3 bags and 1 instrument box and pays a sum of ₹ 160. From the same shop, Vikram buys 2 pens, 1 bag and 3 instrument boxes and pays a sum of ₹ 190. Also Ankur buys 1 pen, 2 bags and 4 instrument boxes and pays a sum of ₹ 250.

  1. Convert the given above situation into a matrix equation of the form AX = B. (1)
  2. Find |A|. (1)
  3. Find A-1. (2)
    OR
    Determine P = A2 - 5A. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Gautam buys 5 pens, 3 bags and 1 instrument box and pays a sum of ₹ 160. From the same shop, Vikram buys 2 pens, 1 bag and 3 instrument boxes and pays a sum of ₹ 190. Also Ankur buys 1 pen, 2 bags and 4 instrument boxes and pays a sum of ₹ 250. Convert the given above situation into a matrix equation of the form AX = B. (1) Find |A|. (1) Find A -1 . (2) OR Determine P = A 2 - 5A. (2)
explanation: Matrix equation is AX = B, where A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 160 \\ 190 \\ 250 \end{array}\right]{/tex} where x is the number of pens bought, y the number of bags and z the number of instrument boxes. |A| = 5(4 - 6) - 3(8 - 3) + 1(4 - 1) = -22 adj (A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -5 & 3 \\ -10 & 19 & -7 \\ 8 & -13 & -1 \end{array}\right]^{\prime}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -10 & 8 \\ -5 & 19 & -13 \\ 3 & -7 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{(-22)}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} -2 & -10 & 8 \\ -5 & 19 & -13 \\ 3 & -7 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} OR P = A 2 - 5A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 32 & 20 & 18 \\ 15 & 13 & 17 \\ 13 & 13 & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 25 & 15 & 5 \\ 10 & 5 & 15 \\ 5 & 10 & 20 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 5 & 13 \\ 5 & 8 & 2 \\ 8 & 3 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: Matrix equation is AX = B, where A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 160 \\ 190 \\ 250 \end{array}\right]{/tex} where x is the number of pens bought, y the number of bags and z the number of instrument boxes. |A| = 5(4 - 6) - 3(8 - 3) + 1(4 - 1) = -22 adj (A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -5 & 3 \\ -10 & 19 & -7 \\ 8 & -13 & -1 \end{array}\right]^{\prime}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -10 & 8 \\ -5 & 19 & -13 \\ 3 & -7 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{(-22)}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} -2 & -10 & 8 \\ -5 & 19 & -13 \\ 3 & -7 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} OR P = A 2 - 5A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 32 & 20 & 18 \\ 15 & 13 & 17 \\ 13 & 13 & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 25 & 15 & 5 \\ 10 & 5 & 15 \\ 5 & 10 & 20 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 5 & 13 \\ 5 & 8 & 2 \\ 8 & 3 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Matrix equation is AX = B, where
    A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 160 \\ 190 \\ 250 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    where x is the number of pens bought, y the number of bags and z the number of instrument boxes.

  2. |A| = 5(4 - 6) - 3(8 - 3) + 1(4 - 1) = -22

  3. adj (A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -5 & 3 \\ -10 & 19 & -7 \\ 8 & -13 & -1 \end{array}\right]^{\prime}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -10 & 8 \\ -5 & 19 & -13 \\ 3 & -7 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{(-22)}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} -2 & -10 & 8 \\ -5 & 19 & -13 \\ 3 & -7 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    OR
    P = A2 - 5A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 32 & 20 & 18 \\ 15 & 13 & 17 \\ 13 & 13 & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 25 & 15 & 5 \\ 10 & 5 & 15 \\ 5 & 10 & 20 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 5 & 13 \\ 5 & 8 & 2 \\ 8 & 3 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
The monthly incomes of two sister Rita and Ritika are in the ratio 3 : 4 and their monthly expenditures are in the ratio 5 : 7. Each sister saves ₹ 15,000 per month.
 

  1. What is the system of linear equations representing the given information about the monthly income of Rita and Ritika are ₹3x and ₹4x, respectively and their monthly expenditures are ₹5y and ₹7y, respectively. (1)
  2. What is the matrix equation representing the given system of linear equations? (1)
    3x - 5y = 15000
    4x - 7y = 15000
  3. If A = {tex} \begin{bmatrix}3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} then what is the value of A-1? (2)
    OR
    What is the monthly income of Rita and Ritika? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: The monthly incomes of two sister Rita and Ritika are in the ratio 3 : 4 and their monthly expenditures are in the ratio 5 : 7. Each sister saves ₹ 15,000 per month. What is the system of linear equations representing the given information about the monthly income of Rita and Ritika are ₹3x and ₹4x, respectively and their monthly expenditures are ₹5y and ₹7y, respectively. (1) What is the matrix equation representing the given system of linear equations? (1) 3x - 5y = 15000 4x - 7y = 15000 If A = {tex} \begin{bmatrix}3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} then what is the value of A -1 ? (2) OR What is the monthly income of Rita and Ritika? (2)
explanation: Monthly savings of Rita = Monthly income of Rita - Monthly Expenditure of Rita 15000 = 3x - 5y Similarly for Ritika 15000 = 4x - 7y Here required system of linear equation 3x - 5y = 15000 4x - 7y = 15000 We have system of linear equations 3x - 5y = 15000 4x -7y = 15000 This may be written in matrix system as AX = B, where A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} , X = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} , B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} Because, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}3x -5y \\ 4 x -7y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} We have |A| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rr} 3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} |A| = -21 + 20 = -1 A 11 = -7 A 12 = -4 A 21 = 5 A 22 = 3 Adj A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}-7 & -4 \\ 5 & 3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}^{T}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}-7 & 5 \\ -4& 3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj A = {tex}\frac{1}{-1}\begin{bmatrix}-7 & 5 \\ -4& 3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}7 & -5 \\ 4& -3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} OR We have X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}7 & -5 \\ 4 & -3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}105000-75000 \\ 60000-45000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}30000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 30,000, y = 15,000 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rita's monthly income = 3x = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 30000 = ₹ 90000 And Ritika's income = 4x = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 30000 = ₹ 120000
solution: Monthly savings of Rita = Monthly income of Rita - Monthly Expenditure of Rita 15000 = 3x - 5y Similarly for Ritika 15000 = 4x - 7y Here required system of linear equation 3x - 5y = 15000 4x - 7y = 15000 We have system of linear equations 3x - 5y = 15000 4x -7y = 15000 This may be written in matrix system as AX = B, where A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} , X = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} , B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} Because, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}3x -5y \\ 4 x -7y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} We have |A| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rr} 3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} |A| = -21 + 20 = -1 A 11 = -7 A 12 = -4 A 21 = 5 A 22 = 3 Adj A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}-7 & -4 \\ 5 & 3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}^{T}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}-7 & 5 \\ -4& 3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj A = {tex}\frac{1}{-1}\begin{bmatrix}-7 & 5 \\ -4& 3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}7 & -5 \\ 4& -3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} OR We have X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}7 & -5 \\ 4 & -3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}105000-75000 \\ 60000-45000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}30000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 30,000, y = 15,000 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rita's monthly income = 3x = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 30000 = ₹ 90000 And Ritika's income = 4x = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 30000 = ₹ 120000
solution_html:
1. Monthly savings of Rita
    = Monthly income of Rita - Monthly Expenditure of Rita
    15000 = 3x - 5y
    Similarly for Ritika
    15000 = 4x - 7y
    Here required system of linear equation
    3x - 5y = 15000
    4x - 7y = 15000

  2. We have system of linear equations
    3x - 5y = 15000
    4x -7y = 15000
    This may be written in matrix system as
    AX = B, where A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}, X = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex}, B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    Because,
    {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex}{tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}3x -5y \\ 4 x -7y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex}

  3. We have |A| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rr} 3 & -5 \\ 4 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} |A| = -21 + 20 = -1
    A11 = -7 A12 = -4
    A21 = 5 A22 = 3
    Adj A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}-7 & -4 \\ 5 & 3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}^{T}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}-7 & 5 \\ -4& 3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj A
    = {tex}\frac{1}{-1}\begin{bmatrix}-7 & 5 \\ -4& 3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}7 & -5 \\ 4& -3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    OR
    We have X = A-1 B
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}7 & -5 \\ 4 & -3 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}15000 \\ 15000 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}105000-75000 \\ 60000-45000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}30000 \\ 15000 \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 30,000, y = 15,000
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rita's monthly income = 3x = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 30000 = ₹ 90000
    And Ritika's income = 4x = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 30000 = ₹ 120000

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Three friends Rajat, Raj and Raman went to a vegetable market to purchase vegetable. From a vegetable shop Rajat purchased 1 kg each of Potato, Onion and Brinjal for a total of ₹ 21. Raj purchased 4 kg of potato, 3 kg of onion and 2 kg of Brinjal for ₹ 60 while Raman purchased 6 kg potato, 2 kg onion and 3 kg brinjal for ₹ 70.

  1. What is the algebraic representation of the given situation where the cost of potato is ₹x per kg, the cost of onion is ₹y per kg, and the cost of brinjal is ₹z per kg? (1)
  2. If AX = B, where A, X, B are matrix then X should be (1)
  3. If A = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} then what is the value of A-1? (2)
    OR
    What is the cost of potato, onion, and brinjal? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Three friends Rajat, Raj and Raman went to a vegetable market to purchase vegetable. From a vegetable shop Rajat purchased 1 kg each of Potato, Onion and Brinjal for a total of ₹ 21. Raj purchased 4 kg of potato, 3 kg of onion and 2 kg of Brinjal for ₹ 60 while Raman purchased 6 kg potato, 2 kg onion and 3 kg brinjal for ₹ 70. What is the algebraic representation of the given situation where the cost of potato is ₹x per kg, the cost of onion is ₹y per kg, and the cost of brinjal is ₹z per kg? (1) If AX = B, where A, X, B are matrix then X should be (1) If A = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} then what is the value of A -1 ? (2) OR What is the cost of potato, onion, and brinjal? (2)
explanation: From question: For Rajat x + y + z = 21 For Raj 4x + 3y + 2z = 60 For Raman 6x + 2y + 3z = 70 Therefore, Algebraical representation is x + y + z = 21 4x + 3 y + 2z = 60 6x + 2y + 3z = 70 We have AX = B Pre multiplying by A -1 on both sides, we have A -1 AX = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (A -1 A)X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IX = A -1 B [A -1 A = I (Identity matrix] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = A -1 B [IX = X] |A|= {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 1 (9 - 4) -1 (12 - 12) + (8 -18) = 5 - 0 - 10 = - 5 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 Therefore, A -1 exists. Now, A 11 = 9 - 4 = 5 ; A 21 = - ( 3 - 2 ) = - 1 ; A 31 = 2 - 3 = -1 A 12 = - (12 -12 ) = 0; A 22 =(3 -6) = -3; A 32 = - (2 - 4) = 2 A 13 = (8 -18 ) = -10; A 23 = -(2 - 6) = 4; A 33 = (3 - 4) = -1 {tex}\therefore {/tex} Adj A= {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & -10 \\ -1 & -3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|^{T}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\because {/tex} {tex}A^{-1}=\frac{1}{|A|} \operatorname{Adj} \mathrm{A}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} OR We have X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right|\left|\begin{array}{l} 21 \\ 60 \\ 70 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} -25 \\ -40 \\ -40 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} 5 \\ 8 \\ 8 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5, y = 8, z = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost of potato,onion and brinjal are ₹ 5, ₹ 8 and ₹ 8
solution: From question: For Rajat x + y + z = 21 For Raj 4x + 3y + 2z = 60 For Raman 6x + 2y + 3z = 70 Therefore, Algebraical representation is x + y + z = 21 4x + 3 y + 2z = 60 6x + 2y + 3z = 70 We have AX = B Pre multiplying by A -1 on both sides, we have A -1 AX = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (A -1 A)X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IX = A -1 B [A -1 A = I (Identity matrix] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = A -1 B [IX = X] |A|= {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 1 (9 - 4) -1 (12 - 12) + (8 -18) = 5 - 0 - 10 = - 5 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 Therefore, A -1 exists. Now, A 11 = 9 - 4 = 5 ; A 21 = - ( 3 - 2 ) = - 1 ; A 31 = 2 - 3 = -1 A 12 = - (12 -12 ) = 0; A 22 =(3 -6) = -3; A 32 = - (2 - 4) = 2 A 13 = (8 -18 ) = -10; A 23 = -(2 - 6) = 4; A 33 = (3 - 4) = -1 {tex}\therefore {/tex} Adj A= {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & -10 \\ -1 & -3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|^{T}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\because {/tex} {tex}A^{-1}=\frac{1}{|A|} \operatorname{Adj} \mathrm{A}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} OR We have X = A -1 B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right|\left|\begin{array}{l} 21 \\ 60 \\ 70 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} -25 \\ -40 \\ -40 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} 5 \\ 8 \\ 8 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5, y = 8, z = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost of potato,onion and brinjal are ₹ 5, ₹ 8 and ₹ 8
solution_html:
1. From question:
    For Rajat x + y + z = 21
    For Raj 4x + 3y + 2z = 60
    For Raman 6x + 2y + 3z = 70
    Therefore, Algebraical representation is
    x + y + z = 21
    4x + 3 y + 2z = 60
    6x + 2y + 3z = 70

  2. We have AX = B
    Pre multiplying by A-1 on both sides, we have
    A-1 AX = A-1B
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}(A-1 A)X = A-1B
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}IX = A-1 B [A -1A = I (Identity matrix]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}X = A-1 B [IX = X]

  3. |A|={tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex}= 1 (9 - 4) -1 (12 - 12) + (8 -18)
    = 5 - 0 - 10 = - 5 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
    Therefore, A-1 exists.
    Now, A11 = 9 - 4 = 5 ; A21 = - ( 3 - 2 ) = - 1 ; A31 = 2 - 3 = -1
    A12 = - (12 -12 ) = 0; A22 =(3 -6) = -3; A32 = - (2 - 4) = 2
    A13 = (8 -18 ) = -10; A23 = -(2 - 6) = 4; A33 = (3 - 4) = -1
    {tex}\therefore {/tex}Adj A={tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & -10 \\ -1 & -3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|^{T}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    {tex}\because {/tex}{tex}A^{-1}=\frac{1}{|A|} \operatorname{Adj} \mathrm{A}{/tex}
    = {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex}{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    OR

    We have X = A-1B
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right|{/tex}={tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex}{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & 2 \\ -10 & 4 & -1 \end{array}\right|\left|\begin{array}{l} 21 \\ 60 \\ 70 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right|{/tex}= {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex}{tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} -25 \\ -40 \\ -40 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right|{/tex}= {tex}\left|\begin{array}{l} 5 \\ 8 \\ 8 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5, y = 8, z = 8
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost of potato,onion and brinjal are ₹ 5, ₹ 8 and ₹ 8

question_html:
Area of a triangle whose vertices are (x1, y1), (x2, y2) and (x3, y3) is given by the determinant
{tex}\Delta=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x_{1} & y_{1} & 1 \\ x_{2} & y_{2} & 1 \\ x_{3} & y_{3} & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
Since, area is a positive quantity, so we always take the absolute value of the determinant A. Also, the area of the triangle formed by three collinear points is zero.

  1. Find the area of the triangle whose vertices are (-2, 6), (3, -6), and (1, 5). (1)
  2. If the points (2, -3), (k, -1) and (0, 4) are collinear, then find the value of 4k. (1)
  3. If the area of a triangle ABC, with vertices A(1, 3), B(0, 0) and C(k, 0) is 3 sq. units, then find the value of k. (2)
    OR
    Using determinants, find the equation of the line joining the points A(1, 2) and B(3, 6). (2)
question_text: Area of a triangle whose vertices are (x 1 , y 1 ), (x 2 , y 2 ) and (x 3 , y 3 ) is given by the determinant {tex}\Delta=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x_{1} & y_{1} & 1 \\ x_{2} & y_{2} & 1 \\ x_{3} & y_{3} & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Since, area is a positive quantity, so we always take the absolute value of the determinant A. Also, the area of the triangle formed by three collinear points is zero. Find the area of the triangle whose vertices are (-2, 6), (3, -6), and (1, 5). (1) If the points (2, -3), (k, -1) and (0, 4) are collinear, then find the value of 4k. (1) If the area of a triangle ABC, with vertices A(1, 3), B(0, 0) and C(k, 0) is 3 sq. units, then find the value of k. (2) OR Using determinants, find the equation of the line joining the points A(1, 2) and B(3, 6). (2)
explanation: Let {tex}\Delta{/tex} be the area of the triangle then, {tex}\Delta = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&6&1 \\ 3&{ - 6}&1 \\ 1&5&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |-2(-6 - 5) - 6(3 - 1) + 1(15 + 6)| [Expanding along R 1 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |43 - 12| = 15.5 sq. units The given points are collinear. {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 1 \\ k & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 Expanding along R 1 , we get 2(-1 - 4) + 3(k) + 1(4k) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7k - 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{10}{7} \Rightarrow{/tex} 4k = {tex}\frac{40}{7}{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = 3 sq. units [Given] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ k & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex} 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ k & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex} 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(0 - 0) - 3(0 - k) + 1(0 - 0) = {tex}\pm{/tex} 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3k = {tex}\pm{/tex} 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2 OR Let Q(x, y) be any point on the line joining A(1, 2) and B(3, 6). Then, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABQ = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \\ x & y & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(6 - y) - 2(3 - x) + 1(3y - 6x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 - y - 6 + 2x + 3y - 6x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4x = -2y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = y
solution: Let {tex}\Delta{/tex} be the area of the triangle then, {tex}\Delta = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&6&1 \\ 3&{ - 6}&1 \\ 1&5&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |-2(-6 - 5) - 6(3 - 1) + 1(15 + 6)| [Expanding along R 1 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |43 - 12| = 15.5 sq. units The given points are collinear. {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 1 \\ k & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 Expanding along R 1 , we get 2(-1 - 4) + 3(k) + 1(4k) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7k - 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{10}{7} \Rightarrow{/tex} 4k = {tex}\frac{40}{7}{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = 3 sq. units [Given] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ k & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex} 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ k & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex} 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(0 - 0) - 3(0 - k) + 1(0 - 0) = {tex}\pm{/tex} 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3k = {tex}\pm{/tex} 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2 OR Let Q(x, y) be any point on the line joining A(1, 2) and B(3, 6). Then, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABQ = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \\ x & y & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(6 - y) - 2(3 - x) + 1(3y - 6x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 - y - 6 + 2x + 3y - 6x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4x = -2y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = y
solution_html:
1. Let {tex}\Delta{/tex} be the area of the triangle then,
    {tex}\Delta = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&6&1 \\ 3&{ - 6}&1 \\ 1&5&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |-2(-6 - 5) - 6(3 - 1) + 1(15 + 6)| [Expanding along R1]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |43 - 12| = 15.5 sq. units
  2. The given points are collinear.
    {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 1 \\ k & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
    Expanding along R1, we get
    2(-1 - 4) + 3(k) + 1(4k) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7k - 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{10}{7} \Rightarrow{/tex} 4k = {tex}\frac{40}{7}{/tex}
  3. Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = 3 sq. units [Given]
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ k & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex}3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ k & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex}6
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(0 - 0) - 3(0 - k) + 1(0 - 0) = {tex}\pm{/tex}6
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3k = {tex}\pm{/tex}6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\pm{/tex}2
    OR
    Let Q(x, y) be any point on the line joining A(1, 2) and B(3, 6). Then, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABQ = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \\ x & y & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1(6 - y) - 2(3 - x) + 1(3y - 6x) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 - y - 6 + 2x + 3y - 6x = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4x = -2y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = y

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Minor of an element aij of a determinant is the determinant obtained by deleting its ith row and jth column in which element aij lies and is denoted by Mij.
Cofactor of an element aij, denoted by Aij, is defined by Aij = (-1)i+j Mij, where Mij is minor of aij. Also, the determinant of a square matrix A is the sum of the products of the elements of any row (or column) with their corresponding cofactors. For example, if A = [aij]3{tex}\times{/tex}3, then |A| = a11A11 + a12A12 + a13A13.

  1. What is the sum of the cofactors of all the elements of {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 4 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex}? (1)
  2. What is the minor of a21 of {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 6 & -3 \\ -4 & 3 & 2 \\ -4 & -7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex}? (1)
  3. In the given determinant {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 5 \\ 6 & 0 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex}, what is the value of the element at position of a32 {tex}\cdot{/tex} A32? (2)
    OR
    If {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 3 & 8 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex}, then what is the minor of a23? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Minor of an element a ij of a determinant is the determinant obtained by deleting its i th row and j th column in which element a ij lies and is denoted by M ij . Cofactor of an element a ij , denoted by A ij , is defined by A ij = (-1) i+j M ij , where M ij is minor of a ij . Also, the determinant of a square matrix A is the sum of the products of the elements of any row (or column) with their corresponding cofactors. For example, if A = [a ij ] 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 , then |A| = a 11 A 11 + a 12 A 12 + a 13 A 13 . What is the sum of the cofactors of all the elements of {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 4 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} ? (1) What is the minor of a 21 of {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 6 & -3 \\ -4 & 3 & 2 \\ -4 & -7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} ? (1) In the given determinant {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 5 \\ 6 & 0 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex} , what is the value of the element at position of a 32 {tex}\cdot{/tex} A 32 ? (2) OR If {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 3 & 8 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} , then what is the minor of a 23 ? (2)
explanation: Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 4 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Cofactor of 1 = 3, cofactor of -2 = -4, Cofactor of 4 = 2, cofactor of 3 = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required sum = 3 - 4 + 2 + 1 = 2 Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 6 & -3 \\ -4 & 3 & 2 \\ -4 & -7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Minor of a 21 = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 6 & -3 \\ -7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 18 - 21 = -3 Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 5 \\ 6 & 0 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Clearly, a 32 = 5 and A 32 = cofactor of a 32 in {tex}\Delta{/tex} = (-1) 3+2 {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 6 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = (-1) (8 - 30) = 22 {tex}\therefore{/tex} a 32 {tex}\cdot{/tex} A 32 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 22 = 110 OR Here, {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 3 & 8 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Minor of a 23 = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 10 - 3 = 7
solution: Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 4 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Cofactor of 1 = 3, cofactor of -2 = -4, Cofactor of 4 = 2, cofactor of 3 = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required sum = 3 - 4 + 2 + 1 = 2 Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 6 & -3 \\ -4 & 3 & 2 \\ -4 & -7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Minor of a 21 = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 6 & -3 \\ -7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 18 - 21 = -3 Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 5 \\ 6 & 0 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Clearly, a 32 = 5 and A 32 = cofactor of a 32 in {tex}\Delta{/tex} = (-1) 3+2 {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 6 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = (-1) (8 - 30) = 22 {tex}\therefore{/tex} a 32 {tex}\cdot{/tex} A 32 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 22 = 110 OR Here, {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 3 & 8 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Minor of a 23 = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 10 - 3 = 7
solution_html:
1. Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 4 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    Cofactor of 1 = 3, cofactor of -2 = -4, Cofactor of 4 = 2, cofactor of 3 = 1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required sum = 3 - 4 + 2 + 1 = 2

  2. Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 6 & -3 \\ -4 & 3 & 2 \\ -4 & -7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    Minor of a21 = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 6 & -3 \\ -7 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 18 - 21 = -3

  3. Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 5 \\ 6 & 0 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 & -7 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    Clearly, a32 = 5
    and A32 = cofactor of a32 in {tex}\Delta{/tex} = (-1)3+2 {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 6 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    = (-1) (8 - 30) = 22
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} a32 {tex}\cdot{/tex} A32 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 22 = 110
    OR
    Here, {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 3 & 8 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Minor of a23 = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 10 - 3 = 7

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Reena wants to donate a rectangular plot of land for a school of her village. When she was asked by construction agency to give dimensions of the plot, she said that if its length is decreased by 50 m and breadth is increased by 50 m, then its area will remain same, but if length is decreased by 10 m and breadth is decreased by 20 m, then its area will decrease by 5300 m2.
 

  1. In a rectangular plot of land, if the length is represented as "x" meters and the breadth is represented as "y" meters, how can the situation described be expressed in the form of a system of linear equations? (1)

  2. Given the system of linear equations: (1)
    x - y = 50
    2x + y = 550
    How can we express this system of equations in the form of a matrix equation?

  3. If A = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} then what is the value of A-1? (2)
    OR
    Reena donated a rectangular plot of land to the school. Can you please provide the dimensions of the plot in terms of its length and breadth? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Reena wants to donate a rectangular plot of land for a school of her village. When she was asked by construction agency to give dimensions of the plot, she said that if its length is decreased by 50 m and breadth is increased by 50 m, then its area will remain same, but if length is decreased by 10 m and breadth is decreased by 20 m, then its area will decrease by 5300 m 2 . In a rectangular plot of land, if the length is represented as "x" meters and the breadth is represented as "y" meters, how can the situation described be expressed in the form of a system of linear equations? (1) Given the system of linear equations: (1) x - y = 50 2x + y = 550 How can we express this system of equations in the form of a matrix equation? If A = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} then what is the value of A -1 ? (2) OR Reena donated a rectangular plot of land to the school. Can you please provide the dimensions of the plot in terms of its length and breadth? (2)
explanation: (x - 50)(y + 50) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 50x - 50y = 2500 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y = 50 Also (x - 10)(y - 20) = xy - 5300 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 20x - 10y + 200 = xy - 5300 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 20x - 10y = -5300 - 200 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + y = 550 Hence, represented system of linear equations is x - y = 50 2x + y = 550 We can write matrix system as AX = B, where A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} , X = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} x \\[0.3em] y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} , B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\[0.3em] 550 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} Because {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix} x \\[0.3em] y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\[0.3em] 550 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \begin{bmatrix} x-y \\[0.3em] 2x+y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\[0.3em] 550 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y = 50 2x + y = 550 We have A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| = 1 + 2 = 3 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Now, A 11 = 1, A 12 = -2 A 21 = 1, A 22 = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} adj A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} ^{T}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj A = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex} OR We have {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = A -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y \end{bmatrix} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ -2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 550 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}600\\ 450\end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}200\\150 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 200, y = 150 Hence dimension is 200 m and 150 m
solution: (x - 50)(y + 50) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 50x - 50y = 2500 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y = 50 Also (x - 10)(y - 20) = xy - 5300 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 20x - 10y + 200 = xy - 5300 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 20x - 10y = -5300 - 200 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + y = 550 Hence, represented system of linear equations is x - y = 50 2x + y = 550 We can write matrix system as AX = B, where A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} , X = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} x \\[0.3em] y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} , B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\[0.3em] 550 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} Because {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix} x \\[0.3em] y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\[0.3em] 550 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \begin{bmatrix} x-y \\[0.3em] 2x+y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\[0.3em] 550 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y = 50 2x + y = 550 We have A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| = 1 + 2 = 3 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Now, A 11 = 1, A 12 = -2 A 21 = 1, A 22 = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} adj A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} ^{T}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj A = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex} OR We have {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = A -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y \end{bmatrix} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ -2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 550 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}600\\ 450\end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}200\\150 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 200, y = 150 Hence dimension is 200 m and 150 m
solution_html:
1. (x - 50)(y + 50) = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 50x - 50y = 2500 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y = 50
    Also (x - 10)(y - 20) = xy - 5300
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 20x - 10y + 200 = xy - 5300
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 20x - 10y = -5300 - 200
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + y = 550
    Hence, represented system of linear equations is
    x - y = 50
    2x + y = 550

  2. We can write matrix system as
    AX = B, where A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}, X = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} x \\[0.3em] y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}, B = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\[0.3em] 550 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    Because {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex}{tex}\begin{bmatrix} x \\[0.3em] y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\[0.3em] 550 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \begin{bmatrix} x-y \\[0.3em] 2x+y \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\[0.3em] 550 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y = 50
    2x + y = 550

  3. We have
    A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |A| = 1 + 2 = 3 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
    Now, A11 = 1, A12 = -2
    A21 = 1, A22 = 1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} adj A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} ^{T}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj A = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}{tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex}
    OR
    We have
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = A-1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y \end{bmatrix} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}{tex}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ -2 & 1 \\[0.3em] \end{bmatrix} {/tex}{tex}\left|\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 550 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}{tex}\begin{bmatrix}600\\ 450\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y \end{bmatrix}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}200\\150 \end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 200, y = 150
    Hence dimension is
    200 m and 150 m

question_html:
The month of September is celebrated as the Rashtriya Poshan Maah across the country. Following a healthy and well-balanced diet is crucial in order to supply the body with the proper nutrients it needs. A balanced diet also keeps us mentally fit and promotes improved level of energy.

Figure - 1

Figure - 2

A dietician wishes to minimize the cost of a diet involving two types of foods, food {tex}{X}({x} {kg}){/tex} and food {tex}{Y}({y} {kg}){/tex} which are available at the rate of ₹ {tex}16 / {kg}{/tex} and ₹ {tex}20 / {kg}{/tex} respectively. The feasible region satisfying the constraints is shown in Figure-2.

On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Identify and write all the constraints which determine the given feasible region in Figure-2. (2)
  2. If the objective is to minimize cost Z = 16x + 20y, find the values of x and y at which cost is minimum. Also, find minimum cost assuming that minimum cost is possible for the given unbounded region. (2)
question_text: The month of September is celebrated as the Rashtriya Poshan Maah across the country. Following a healthy and well-balanced diet is crucial in order to supply the body with the proper nutrients it needs. A balanced diet also keeps us mentally fit and promotes improved level of energy. Figure - 1 Figure - 2 A dietician wishes to minimize the cost of a diet involving two types of foods, food {tex}{X}({x} {kg}){/tex} and food {tex}{Y}({y} {kg}){/tex} which are available at the rate of ₹ {tex}16 / {kg}{/tex} and ₹ {tex}20 / {kg}{/tex} respectively. The feasible region satisfying the constraints is shown in Figure-2. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: Identify and write all the constraints which determine the given feasible region in Figure-2. (2) If the objective is to minimize cost Z = 16x + 20y, find the values of x and y at which cost is minimum. Also, find minimum cost assuming that minimum cost is possible for the given unbounded region. (2)
explanation: {tex} \text { (Constraints are } x+2 y \geq 10 {/tex} {tex} x+y \geq 6 {/tex} {tex} 3 x+y \geq 8 {/tex} {tex} x \geq 0 {/tex} {tex} y \geq 0 {/tex} Corner points Value of {tex}Z=16 x+20 y{/tex} {tex}{~A}(10,0){/tex} 160 {tex}{~B}(2,4){/tex} 112 {tex}{C}(1,5){/tex} 116 {tex}{D}(0,8){/tex} 160 The minimum cost is ₹ 112
solution: {tex} \text { (Constraints are } x+2 y \geq 10 {/tex} {tex} x+y \geq 6 {/tex} {tex} 3 x+y \geq 8 {/tex} {tex} x \geq 0 {/tex} {tex} y \geq 0 {/tex} Corner points Value of {tex}Z=16 x+20 y{/tex} {tex}{~A}(10,0){/tex} 160 {tex}{~B}(2,4){/tex} 112 {tex}{C}(1,5){/tex} 116 {tex}{D}(0,8){/tex} 160 The minimum cost is ₹ 112
solution_html:
1.  {tex} \text { (Constraints are } x+2 y \geq 10 {/tex}
    {tex} x+y \geq 6 {/tex}
    {tex} 3 x+y \geq 8 {/tex}
    {tex} x \geq 0 {/tex}
    {tex} y \geq 0 {/tex}
  2. Corner points Value of {tex}Z=16 x+20 y{/tex}
    {tex}{~A}(10,0){/tex} 160
    {tex}{~B}(2,4){/tex} 112
    {tex}{C}(1,5){/tex} 116
    {tex}{D}(0,8){/tex} 160
    The minimum cost is ₹ 112

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
The Palace of Peace and Reconciliation, also known as the Pyramid of peace and Accord is a 62-metre high Pyramid in Mursultan, the capital of Kazakhstan, that serves as a non-denominational national spiritual centre and an event venue. It is designed by faster and partners with a stained glass apex. It has 25 smaller equilateral triangles as shown in the figure.

  1. If the vertices of one triangle are (0, 0), (3, {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}) and (3, {tex}-\sqrt3{/tex}) then find the area. (1)
  2. Find the area of a face of the Pyramid. (1)
  3. Find the lenght of a altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle. (2)
    OR
    Using determinants, find the equation of the line joining the points A(1, 2) and B(3, 6). (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: The Palace of Peace and Reconciliation, also known as the Pyramid of peace and Accord is a 62-metre high Pyramid in Mursultan, the capital of Kazakhstan, that serves as a non-denominational national spiritual centre and an event venue. It is designed by faster and partners with a stained glass apex. It has 25 smaller equilateral triangles as shown in the figure. If the vertices of one triangle are (0, 0), (3, {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} ) and (3, {tex}-\sqrt3{/tex} ) then find the area. (1) Find the area of a face of the Pyramid. (1) Find the lenght of a altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle. (2) OR Using determinants, find the equation of the line joining the points A(1, 2) and B(3, 6). (2)
explanation: Required Area = {tex}\left|\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & \sqrt{3} & 1 \\ 3 & -\sqrt{3} & 1 \end{array}\right|\right|{/tex} = {tex}\mid \frac{1}{2}[1(-3 \sqrt{3}-3 \sqrt{3})] \mid{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{2}=3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} sq units Since, a face of the Pyтamid consists of 25 smaller equilateral triangles. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of a face of the Pyramid = {tex}25 \times 3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}75 \sqrt{3}{/tex} sq. units. Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} (side) 2 {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} {/tex} (side) 2 [As calculated above area of equilateral triangle is {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} sq. units] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (side) 2 = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} side = {tex}2 \sqrt{3} {/tex} (units) Let h be the length of the altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle. Then, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height = {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} side {tex}\times{/tex} height = {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} or, height = {tex}\frac{3 \sqrt{3} \times 2}{2 \sqrt{3}}{/tex} = 3 units. OR Let the third point on the line be (x, y). The area of triangle with vertices (x, y), (1, 2), (3, 6) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & y & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Since the three points are collinear, the area formed will be zero. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & y & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(2 - 6) - y(1 - 3) + 1(6 - 6) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4x + 2y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - y = 0 Hence, the equation of line joining (1, 2) and (3, 6) is 2x - y = 0
solution: Required Area = {tex}\left|\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & \sqrt{3} & 1 \\ 3 & -\sqrt{3} & 1 \end{array}\right|\right|{/tex} = {tex}\mid \frac{1}{2}[1(-3 \sqrt{3}-3 \sqrt{3})] \mid{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{2}=3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} sq units Since, a face of the Pyтamid consists of 25 smaller equilateral triangles. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of a face of the Pyramid = {tex}25 \times 3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}75 \sqrt{3}{/tex} sq. units. Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} (side) 2 {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} {/tex} (side) 2 [As calculated above area of equilateral triangle is {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} sq. units] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (side) 2 = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} side = {tex}2 \sqrt{3} {/tex} (units) Let h be the length of the altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle. Then, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height = {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} side {tex}\times{/tex} height = {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} or, height = {tex}\frac{3 \sqrt{3} \times 2}{2 \sqrt{3}}{/tex} = 3 units. OR Let the third point on the line be (x, y). The area of triangle with vertices (x, y), (1, 2), (3, 6) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & y & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} Since the three points are collinear, the area formed will be zero. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & y & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(2 - 6) - y(1 - 3) + 1(6 - 6) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4x + 2y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - y = 0 Hence, the equation of line joining (1, 2) and (3, 6) is 2x - y = 0
solution_html:
1. Required Area = {tex}\left|\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & \sqrt{3} & 1 \\ 3 & -\sqrt{3} & 1 \end{array}\right|\right|{/tex}
    = {tex}\mid \frac{1}{2}[1(-3 \sqrt{3}-3 \sqrt{3})] \mid{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{2}=3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} sq units

  2. Since, a face of the Pyтamid consists of 25 smaller equilateral triangles.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of a face of the Pyramid = {tex}25 \times 3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}75 \sqrt{3}{/tex} sq. units.

  3. Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} (side)2
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} {/tex} (side)2 [As calculated above area of equilateral triangle is {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} sq. units]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (side)2 = 12
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} side = {tex}2 \sqrt{3} {/tex} (units)
    Let h be the length of the altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle.
    Then, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height = {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
    or, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} side {tex}\times{/tex} height = {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
    or, height = {tex}\frac{3 \sqrt{3} \times 2}{2 \sqrt{3}}{/tex} = 3 units.
    OR
    Let the third point on the line be (x, y).
    The area of triangle with vertices (x, y), (1, 2), (3, 6)
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & y & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    Since the three points are collinear, the area formed will be zero.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & y & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(2 - 6) - y(1 - 3) + 1(6 - 6) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4x + 2y = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - y = 0
    Hence, the equation of line joining (1, 2) and (3, 6) is 2x - y = 0

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A factory produces three products every day. Their production on a particular day is 45 tons. It is found that production of third product exceeds the production of first product by 8 tons. while the total production of first and third product is twice the production of second product.

  1. It x, y and z respectively denotes the quantity (in tons) of first, second and third product produced, then construct the system of equations and write it in matrix form. (1)
  2. If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right]^{-1}=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}, then find the inverse of {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}. (1)
  3. Find x : y : z. (2)
    OR
    Let P and Q be 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrices such that p {tex}\neq{/tex} Q, If P3 = Q3 and P2Q = Q2P then find determinant of (P2 + Q2). (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A factory produces three products every day. Their production on a particular day is 45 tons. It is found that production of third product exceeds the production of first product by 8 tons. while the total production of first and third product is twice the production of second product. It x, y and z respectively denotes the quantity (in tons) of first, second and third product produced, then construct the system of equations and write it in matrix form. (1) If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right]^{-1}=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex} , then find the inverse of {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex} . (1) Find x : y : z. (2) OR Let P and Q be 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrices such that p {tex}\neq{/tex} Q, If P 3 = Q 3 and P 2 Q = Q 2 P then find determinant of (P 2 + Q 2 ). (2)
explanation: According to given information, the system of linear equations is: x + y + z = 45 z = x + 8 x + z = 2y or, x + y + z = 45 -x + z = 8 x - 2y + z = 0 In matrix form, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 45 \\ 8 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Then A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We know that, (A') -1 = (A -1 )' or, (A -1 )' = (A')' -1 {tex}\therefore \left(A^{-1}\right)'{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & -\frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{1}{6} & \frac{-1}{3} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \\ \frac{1}{3} & 0 & \frac{-1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{3} & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]^{-1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \\ \frac{1}{3} & 0 & \frac{-1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{3} & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 1 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 45 \\ 8 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 90-24+0 \\ 90+0+0 \\ 90+24+0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{c} 66 \\ 90 \\ 114 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 11, y = 15 and z = 19 Required Ratio is 11 : 15 : 19. OR Given, P 3 = Q 3 and P 2 Q = Q 2 P On subtracting, we get P 3 - P 2 Q = Q 3 - Q 2 P {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} P 2 (P - Q) + Q 2 (P - Q) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} (P 2 + Q 2 ) (P - Q) = 0 If |P 2 + Q 2 | {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, then P 2 + Q 2 is invertible. {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} P - Q = 0 contradiction {tex}\therefore {/tex} |P 2 + Q 2 | = 0
solution: According to given information, the system of linear equations is: x + y + z = 45 z = x + 8 x + z = 2y or, x + y + z = 45 -x + z = 8 x - 2y + z = 0 In matrix form, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 45 \\ 8 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Then A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We know that, (A') -1 = (A -1 )' or, (A -1 )' = (A')' -1 {tex}\therefore \left(A^{-1}\right)'{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & -\frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{1}{6} & \frac{-1}{3} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \\ \frac{1}{3} & 0 & \frac{-1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{3} & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]^{-1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \\ \frac{1}{3} & 0 & \frac{-1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{3} & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 1 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 45 \\ 8 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 90-24+0 \\ 90+0+0 \\ 90+24+0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{c} 66 \\ 90 \\ 114 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 11, y = 15 and z = 19 Required Ratio is 11 : 15 : 19. OR Given, P 3 = Q 3 and P 2 Q = Q 2 P On subtracting, we get P 3 - P 2 Q = Q 3 - Q 2 P {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} P 2 (P - Q) + Q 2 (P - Q) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} (P 2 + Q 2 ) (P - Q) = 0 If |P 2 + Q 2 | {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, then P 2 + Q 2 is invertible. {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} P - Q = 0 contradiction {tex}\therefore {/tex} |P 2 + Q 2 | = 0
solution_html:
1. According to given information, the system of linear equations is:
    x + y + z = 45
    z = x + 8
    x + z = 2y
    or, x + y + z = 45
    -x + z = 8
    x - 2y + z = 0
    In matrix form,
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 45 \\ 8 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

  2. Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Then A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    We know that,
    (A')-1 = (A-1)'
    or, (A-1)' = (A')'-1
    {tex}\therefore \left(A^{-1}\right)'{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & -\frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{1}{6} & \frac{-1}{3} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \\ \frac{1}{3} & 0 & \frac{-1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{3} & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]^{-1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \\ \frac{1}{3} & 0 & \frac{-1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{3} & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

  3. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 1 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 45 \\ 8 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 90-24+0 \\ 90+0+0 \\ 90+24+0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{c} 66 \\ 90 \\ 114 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 11, y = 15 and z = 19
    Required Ratio is 11 : 15 : 19.
    OR
    Given, P3 = Q3 and P2Q = Q2P
    On subtracting, we get
    P3 - P2Q = Q3 - Q2P
    {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} P2(P - Q) + Q2(P - Q) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} (P2 + Q2) (P - Q) = 0
    If |P2 + Q2| {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, then P2 + Q2 is invertible.
    {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} P - Q = 0 contradiction
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} |P2 + Q2| = 0

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Each triangular face of the Pyramid of Peace in Kazakhstan is made up of 25 smaller equilateral triangles as shown in the figure.

  1. If the vertices of one of the smaller equilateral triangle are (0, 0), (3, {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}) and (3, - {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}), then find the area of such triangle. (1)
  2. Find the area of a face of the Pyramid. (1)
  3. Find the length of a altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle. (2)
    OR
    Let A(a, 0), B(0, b) and C(1, 1) be three points. If {tex}\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}{/tex} = 1, then find the relation between three points.(2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Each triangular face of the Pyramid of Peace in Kazakhstan is made up of 25 smaller equilateral triangles as shown in the figure. If the vertices of one of the smaller equilateral triangle are (0, 0), (3, {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} ) and (3, - {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} ), then find the area of such triangle. (1) Find the area of a face of the Pyramid. (1) Find the length of a altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle. (2) OR Let A(a, 0), B(0, b) and C(1, 1) be three points. If {tex}\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}{/tex} = 1, then find the relation between three points.(2)
explanation: Area of triangle is given by {tex}\left| {\frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{x_1}}&{{y_1}}&1 \\ {{x_2}}&{{y_2}}&1 \\ {{x_3}}&{{y_3}}&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = {tex}\left| {\frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{0}}&{{0}}&1 \\ {{3}}&{{\sqrt3}}&1 \\ {{3}}&{{-\sqrt3}}&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{1}{2}[1(-3 \sqrt{3}-3 \sqrt{3})]\right|{/tex} [Expanding along R 1 ] = 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} sq. units. Since a face of the Pyramid consist of 25 smaller equilateral triangles. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} = 75 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} sq. units. Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} a 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} a 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 2 = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} Let h be the length of altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle. Then, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height = 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} h = 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 3 units OR Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & b & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [a (b - 1) - 0 + 1 (0 - b)] = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (ab - a - b) = 0 [ {tex}\because \frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b + a = ab] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Points A, B and C are collinear.
solution: Area of triangle is given by {tex}\left| {\frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{x_1}}&{{y_1}}&1 \\ {{x_2}}&{{y_2}}&1 \\ {{x_3}}&{{y_3}}&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = {tex}\left| {\frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{0}}&{{0}}&1 \\ {{3}}&{{\sqrt3}}&1 \\ {{3}}&{{-\sqrt3}}&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{1}{2}[1(-3 \sqrt{3}-3 \sqrt{3})]\right|{/tex} [Expanding along R 1 ] = 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} sq. units. Since a face of the Pyramid consist of 25 smaller equilateral triangles. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} = 75 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} sq. units. Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} a 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} a 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 2 = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} Let h be the length of altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle. Then, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height = 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} h = 3 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 3 units OR Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & b & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [a (b - 1) - 0 + 1 (0 - b)] = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (ab - a - b) = 0 [ {tex}\because \frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b + a = ab] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Points A, B and C are collinear.
solution_html:
1. Area of triangle is given by {tex}\left| {\frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{x_1}}&{{y_1}}&1 \\ {{x_2}}&{{y_2}}&1 \\ {{x_3}}&{{y_3}}&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = {tex}\left| {\frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{0}}&{{0}}&1 \\ {{3}}&{{\sqrt3}}&1 \\ {{3}}&{{-\sqrt3}}&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex}
    = {tex}\left|\frac{1}{2}[1(-3 \sqrt{3}-3 \sqrt{3})]\right|{/tex} [Expanding along R1]
    = 3{tex}\sqrt3{/tex} sq. units.

  2. Since a face of the Pyramid consist of 25 smaller equilateral triangles.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 3{tex}\sqrt3{/tex} = 75{tex}\sqrt3{/tex} sq. units.

  3. Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} a2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3{tex}\sqrt3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} a2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2{tex}\sqrt3{/tex}
    Let h be the length of altitude of a smaller equilateral triangle. Then,
    {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height = 3{tex}\sqrt3{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} h = 3{tex}\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 3 units
    OR
    Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & b & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [a (b - 1) - 0 + 1 (0 - b)] = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (ab - a - b) = 0 [{tex}\because \frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b + a = ab]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Points A, B and C are collinear.

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, and U1, U2 are first and second columns respectively of a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix U. Also, let the column matrices U1 and U2 satisfying AU1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and AU2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}.

  1. What is the value of |X| if X is defined as X = [3 2]? (1)
  2. What is the minor of the element at position a22 in matrix U? (1)
  3. What is the result of adding matrices U1 and U2? (2)
    OR
    What is the value of the determinant of matrix U? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , and U 1 , U 2 are first and second columns respectively of a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix U. Also, let the column matrices U 1 and U 2 satisfying AU 1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and AU 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} . What is the value of |X| if X is defined as X = [3 2]? (1) What is the minor of the element at position a 22 in matrix U? (1) What is the result of adding matrices U1 and U2? (2) OR What is the value of the determinant of matrix U? (2)
explanation: We have, X = [3 2] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [3 2] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 7 \\ -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [21 - 16] = [5] {tex}\therefore{/tex} |X| = 5 a 22 in U is -1 and its minor is 1 We have, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let U 1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} a \\ b \end{array}\right]{/tex} then AU 1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} a \\ b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{c} a \\ 2 a+b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 and 2a + b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 and b = -2 Let U 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} c \\ d \end{array}\right]{/tex} then AU 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} c \\ d \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{c} c \\ 2 c+d \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 2 and 2c + d = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 2 and d = 3 - 4 = -1 Thus, U 1 + U 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ -2 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ -1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ -3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} OR Clearly, U = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} |U| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = -1 + 4 = 3
solution: We have, X = [3 2] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [3 2] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 7 \\ -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [21 - 16] = [5] {tex}\therefore{/tex} |X| = 5 a 22 in U is -1 and its minor is 1 We have, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let U 1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} a \\ b \end{array}\right]{/tex} then AU 1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} a \\ b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{c} a \\ 2 a+b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 and 2a + b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 and b = -2 Let U 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} c \\ d \end{array}\right]{/tex} then AU 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} c \\ d \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{c} c \\ 2 c+d \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 2 and 2c + d = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 2 and d = 3 - 4 = -1 Thus, U 1 + U 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ -2 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ -1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ -3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} OR Clearly, U = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} |U| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = -1 + 4 = 3
solution_html:
1. We have, X = [3 2] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    = [3 2] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 7 \\ -8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [21 - 16] = [5]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} |X| = 5

  2. a22 in U is -1 and its minor is 1

  3. We have, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Let U1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} a \\ b \end{array}\right]{/tex} then AU1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} a \\ b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{c} a \\ 2 a+b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 and 2a + b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 and b = -2
    Let U2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} c \\ d \end{array}\right]{/tex} then AU2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} c \\ d \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{c} c \\ 2 c+d \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 2 and 2c + d = 3
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 2 and d = 3 - 4 = -1
    Thus, U1 + U2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ -2 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ -1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ -3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    OR
    Clearly, U = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} |U| = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = -1 + 4 = 3
